title: The End of the Vision Journey
concept: PHASE A CAPSTONE. Concludes the Generative Adversarial Networks phase. The boss exercise explain MUST end with the required handoff: 'Phase A: Generative Adversarial Networks is complete. Next up: NLP Basics.'

exercises.prompt: How many neural networks work together in a standard GAN?
exercises.explain: A GAN is made up of two neural networks: the Generator (which creates data) and the Discriminator (which evaluates data).

cards.title: The Ultimate Showdown
cards.body: You've watched AI generate breathtaking faces, futuristic cars, and imaginary landscapes from scratch. But how does it actually know when its creations are good enough? Let's take a final look at the ultimate showdown that makes this magic happen!

cards.title: What is a GAN?
cards.body: A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) consists of two AI models: the Generator, which creates fake data (like realistic photos) from random noise, and the Discriminator, which acts as a judge trying to distinguish real photos from the fake ones. They train together in a loop until the generated data looks completely real.

cards.title: The Rival Artists
cards.body: Imagine two rival artists: a Counterfeiter who tries to paint realistic masterpieces, and a Detective who studies them to spot fakes. They keep practicing and competing. By the end of this journey, both have become masters: the Counterfeiter paints perfect duplicates, and the Detective can spot even the tiniest errors. This friendly rivalry is exactly how the two parts of a GAN train each other.

cards.title: From Static to Puppies
cards.body: When you train a GAN to create images of puppies, it starts by generating random, colorful static. As the Discriminator points out the mistakes, the Generator adjusts. Eventually, the Generator learns to produce clear, believable pictures of puppies with floppy ears and wet noses.

cards.title: Does one model always win?
cards.body: You might think that because the Discriminator's job is to catch fakes, we want it to win forever. In reality, if the Discriminator is too strong, the Generator never learns. The best GANs result from a perfect, balanced competition where both models grow stronger together.

cards.title: Generator vs. Discriminator
cards.body: Let's compare the roles of the two networks in the GAN framework.

left.text: Creates fake images from scratch to try and fool the judge.

right.text: Inspects images to decide if they are real or generated fakes.

exercises.prompt: What raw input does the Generator use to start creating a new image?

options.text: A list of rules written by humans

exercises.explain: The Generator starts with random noise (random numbers) and transforms it step-by-step into a structured image.

exercises.prompt: True or False: GANs train by having two networks compete against each other.
exercises.explain: Yes! GANs are called 'adversarial' because the Generator and Discriminator compete against each other in a game-like setup to improve.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part of the GAN team:
exercises.sentence: The Generator creates new images, while the [blank] tries to decide if they are real or fake.
exercises.explain: The Discriminator acts as the judge or detective, trying to distinguish between real images and the fake ones created by the Generator.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of these two images is generated by a GAN (Spot the AI):

options.text: Image A: A portrait where the left earlobe is missing and the background is anomalously melted.
Image B: A sharp photograph of a woman with matching earrings and clear background details.
Both are authentic human photographs with zero artifacts.

exercises.explain: AI A is generated by a GAN (it has slight distortions like mismatched earrings or blurred background details common in AI generation), while Image B is a real camera photo.

exercises.prompt: At the very end of training a GAN, what is the ideal final state?

options.text: The Generator produces realistic images, and the Discriminator can only guess with 50% accuracy.
The Discriminator wins easily and rejects all generated images.
The Generator gives up and stops producing images entirely.
Both networks merge together to form a single simple network.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Phase A: Generative Adversarial Networks is complete. Next up: NLP Basics.
